name: C++
description: A powerful systems programming language that extends C with object-oriented, generic, and functional features. Used in game engines, embedded systems, high-frequency trading, and performance-critical applications.
overview: C++ is a statically typed, compiled, multi-paradigm programming language created by Bjarne Stroustrup in 1985 as an enhancement to C. It provides low-level memory control, high-level abstractions, and zero-cost abstractions — you only pay for what you use. C++ powers AAA game engines (Unreal Engine), web browsers (Chrome, Firefox), operating systems, databases (MySQL, MongoDB), financial trading systems, and embedded devices. Modern C++ (C++11 through C++23) adds lambda expressions, smart pointers, move semantics, concurrency support, ranges, coroutines, and modules. With its unmatched combination of performance and expressiveness, C++ remains the language of choice when hardware control and efficiency matter most.

learningPath.title: C++ Fundamentals & Syntax
learningPath.description: Learn C++ core syntax, data types, control flow, functions, and basic I/O. Build a solid foundation in C++ programming.
topics: History of C++ and its evolution (C++98 through C++23)
Setting up development environment (GCC, Clang, MSVC, CMake)
Basic program structure: main(), headers, namespaces
Variables, data types (int, float, double, char, bool, wchar_t)
Operators (arithmetic, relational, logical, bitwise, sizeof, comma)
Control flow: if, else, switch, ternary operator
Loops: for, while, do-while, range-based for (C++11)
Functions: declaration, definition, parameters, return values
Input/output: cin, cout, cerr, file streams, formatting

learningPath.title: Object-Oriented Programming in C++
learningPath.description: Master C++ OOP with classes, inheritance, polymorphism, virtual functions, and access control.
topics: this pointer and const member functions
Copy constructor and copy assignment operator
Rule of Three / Rule of Five (C++11)
Move constructor and move assignment operator (C++11)
Pure virtual functions and abstract base classes
Multiple inheritance and virtual base classes

learningPath.title: Memory Management & Pointers
learningPath.description: Deep dive into C++ memory model, pointers, references, dynamic allocation, smart pointers, and RAII.
topics: Raw pointers vs smart pointers best practices
Pointer to const, const pointer, const pointer to const
Function pointers and member function pointers

learningPath.title: STL: Standard Template Library
learningPath.description: Master the C++ Standard Template Library including containers, iterators, algorithms, and function objects.
topics: Sequence containers: vector, deque, list, forward_list, array
Associative containers: set, multiset, map, multimap
Iterators: input, output, forward, bidirectional, random access
Algorithms: sort, find, binary_search, count, accumulate
Algorithm modifications: copy, remove, replace, transform
projects: Mini search engine with inverted index

learningPath.title: Templates & Generic Programming
learningPath.description: Learn C++ template metaprogramming, variadic templates, concepts, and compile-time computation.
topics: Function templates and template argument deduction
Template parameters: type, non-type, template template
SFINAE (Substitution Failure Is Not An Error)
Compile-time computation with constexpr and consteval (C++20)

learningPath.title: Modern C++ (C++11/14/17/20/23 Features)
learningPath.description: Master modern C++ features that transform how C++ code is written — move semantics, lambdas, ranges, coroutines, and modules.
topics: Lambda expressions: capture by value/reference, generic lambdas
Concepts (C++20) — standard concepts and custom concepts

learningPath.title: File I/O, Exception Handling & Error Management
learningPath.description: Robust error handling, file operations, serialization, and modern error management patterns.
topics: try, catch, throw — best practices and performance
Stack unwinding and RAII during exceptions
std::expected (C++23) for error handling without exceptions
Random access with seekg, seekp, tellg, tellp
projects: File backup utility with error handling

learningPath.title: Concurrency & Multithreading
learningPath.description: Master C++ concurrency including threads, mutexes, condition variables, atomics, and lock-free programming.
topics: Atomic operations: std::atomic, memory ordering (relaxed, acquire, release, acq_rel, seq_cst)
Thread pools: custom implementation and std::thread_pool (C++26)
Parallel algorithms with execution policies (C++17): par, seq, par_unseq
projects: Real-time logging system with async writer

learningPath.title: Advanced C++ & Low-Level Programming
learningPath.description: Deep dive into advanced C++ topics: bit manipulation, inline assembly, network programming, ABI, and performance optimization.
topics: Bit manipulation: bit fields, std::bitset, std::bit_cast (C++20)
ABI (Application Binary Interface) and name mangling
Position Independent Code (PIC) and shared libraries
Cache optimization: cache lines, alignment, prefetching
Compiler explorer (godbolt.org) and understanding generated assembly
Undefined behavior: what it is and how to avoid it

learningPath.title: Design Patterns, Testing & Production C++
learningPath.description: Learn design patterns, testing frameworks, build systems, and best practices for production C++ applications.
topics: Design patterns in C++ (Singleton, Factory, Builder, Observer, Strategy, Visitor, Command)
Idiomatic C++: RAII, PIMPL, CRTP, NVI (Non-Virtual Interface)
Unit testing with Google Test and Google Mock
Build systems: CMake (modern CMake targets, FetchContent, presets)
Code quality: Clang-Tidy, Clang-Format, Cppcheck, sanitizers (ASan, UBSan, MSan, TSan)
C++ coding standards (Google, LLVM, MISRA, AUTOSAR)
Linkers and linking: static libraries, dynamic libraries, symbol visibility
Using C libraries from C++ (extern C, C linkage)
Interfacing with other languages (Python via pybind11, C FFI)
projects: Game engine core with ECS architecture
Database driver with RAII and error handling

interviewQuestions.question: What is the difference between pointer and reference in C++?
interviewQuestions.answer: Pointers can be reassigned and can be null; use * and -> operators; support pointer arithmetic. References cannot be reassigned (bound at initialization), must always refer to a valid object, and use . operator (transparent syntax). References are safer but less flexible than pointers. Use references for function parameters and return values where rebinding is not needed; use pointers when nullability or dynamic allocation is required.

interviewQuestions.question: Explain virtual functions and the vtable.
interviewQuestions.answer: Virtual functions enable runtime polymorphism via dynamic dispatch. The compiler creates a vtable (virtual table) for each class with virtual functions — an array of function pointers. Each object has a vptr (hidden virtual pointer) pointing to its class's vtable. When a virtual function is called through a base pointer/reference, the vptr is used to lookup the correct override at runtime. Virtual functions have performance overhead (indirect call, vtable lookup).

interviewQuestions.question: What is RAII and why is it important?
interviewQuestions.answer: RAII (Resource Acquisition Is Initialization) ties resource lifetime to object lifetime: acquire resource in constructor, release in destructor. This ensures resources (memory, files, mutexes, sockets) are automatically freed when the object goes out of scope, even via exceptions. std::unique_ptr, std::lock_guard, std::ifstream are RAII examples. RAII is fundamental to exception-safe C++ code for leak-free and exception-safe resource management.

interviewQuestions.question: What is the Rule of Five in modern C++?
interviewQuestions.answer: Rule of Five states: if a class needs a custom destructor, copy constructor, copy assignment, move constructor, or move assignment, it usually needs all five. This follows from RAII — custom resource management requires explicit control of copying and moving. Default implementations do shallow copying which causes double-free or resource leaks for owning pointers. = default or = delete explicitly controls these special member functions.

interviewQuestions.question: Explain move semantics and rvalue references.
interviewQuestions.answer: Move semantics (C++11) allow efficiently transferring resources from temporary objects via rvalue references (T&&). std::move converts an lvalue to an rvalue reference, enabling move instead of copy. Move operations steal resources (e.g., swapping pointer, invalidating source) instead of deep copying. This dramatically improves performance for expensive-to-copy objects (vectors, strings) by eliminating unnecessary copies in return values and temporary objects.

interviewQuestions.question: What are smart pointers and when to use each?
interviewQuestions.answer: Smart pointers (C++11) automate memory management: unique_ptr (exclusive ownership, non-copyable, lightweight), shared_ptr (shared ownership via reference counting, heavier), weak_ptr (non-owning observer that doesn't affect ref count, breaks circular references). Use unique_ptr as default for exclusive ownership. Use shared_ptr for shared ownership. Use weak_ptr to observe shared_ptr without extending lifetime. Never use raw owning pointers.

interviewQuestions.question: What is SFINAE and when is it used?
interviewQuestions.answer: SFINAE (Substitution Failure Is Not An Error) is a template metaprogramming principle where substituting template parameters may produce invalid types/code but the compiler doesn't error — it just removes that overload/specialization from consideration. Used with std::enable_if, decltype, and type traits to enable/disable function templates based on type properties. C++20 Concepts provide a more readable alternative to SFINAE.

interviewQuestions.question: Explain the difference between compile-time and runtime polymorphism.
interviewQuestions.answer: Compile-time polymorphism: templates and function overloading — resolved at compile time, no runtime overhead, type-specific code generation. Runtime polymorphism: virtual functions and inheritance — resolved at runtime via vtable, dynamic dispatch overhead, binary interface stability. Templates are type-flexible and faster but increase code size. Virtual functions enable polymorphic behavior across compilation units.

interviewQuestions.question: What is undefined behavior in C++? Give examples.
interviewQuestions.answer: UB means the standard imposes no requirements on the program's behavior — anything can happen (crash, silent corruption, demons flying out of nose). Examples: null pointer dereference, buffer overflow, use-after-free, signed integer overflow, modifying const object, multiple definitions violating ODR, accessing through dangling reference. Tools: -fsanitize=undefined, Valgrind, static analysis. Undefined != unspecified/implementation-defined.

interviewQuestions.question: How do std::move and std::forward work?
interviewQuestions.answer: std::move unconditionally casts an lvalue to an rvalue reference, enabling move semantics. It's just a static_cast<T&&> with appropriate reference collapsing. std::forward conditionally casts to rvalue only if the original argument was an rvalue (perfect forwarding). Used in template functions to preserve argument value category. Both are compile-time casts with no runtime overhead.

interviewQuestions.question: What are C++20 Concepts and how do they improve templates?
interviewQuestions.answer: Concepts specify constraints on template parameters, providing: better error messages (compiler says 'T doesn't satisfy Sortable' instead of 200-line error), overload resolution based on type capabilities, and self-documenting code. Standard concepts: Sortable, Regular, Invocable, etc. Custom concepts use template<...> concept Name = requires(...) { ... }. Concepts replace most SFINAE use cases.

interviewQuestions.question: Explain coroutines in C++20.
interviewQuestions.answer: Coroutines (C++20) are functions that can suspend and resume execution using co_await, co_yield, and co_return. Implemented via promise_type, awaitable objects, and the coroutine handle. Types: generator (produce sequence), task (async computation), and async stream. Coroutines enable efficient async code without callback hell, making them ideal for I/O-bound, event-driven, and generator use cases.

interviewQuestions.question: What is the difference between C++03 and C++11/14/17/20/23?
interviewQuestions.answer: C++11: auto, move semantics, lambdas, smart pointers, threading library, unordered containers. C++14: generic lambdas, constexpr relaxations, binary literals. C++17: structured bindings, if constexpr, filesystem, string_view, variants. C++20: concepts, coroutines, ranges, modules, spans, calendars, format. C++23: std::expected, stacktrace, mdspan, deducing this. Each revision makes C++ safer, more expressive, and easier to use.

interviewQuestions.question: What is the One Definition Rule (ODR)?
interviewQuestions.answer: ODR states: each variable, function, class, enum, template must have exactly one definition in the entire program (across all translation units). Multiple declarations are allowed. Violations cause linker errors (multiple definition) or undefined behavior (if definitions differ). Inline functions (inline keyword or constexpr/consteval) can have multiple identical definitions across TUs. Templates are defined in headers per ODR's allowance.

interviewQuestions.question: How do you prevent memory leaks in C++?
interviewQuestions.answer: Use RAII containers: std::vector, std::string over manual allocation. Use smart pointers (unique_ptr, shared_ptr) for all dynamic allocations. Use containers from STL instead of C-style arrays. Follow Rule of Five for classes managing resources. Use valgrind/sanitizers (ASan, LSan) during development. Never use raw new/delete in application code. Use make_unique/make_shared instead of explicit new.

books.title: The C++ Programming Language
books.author: Bjarne Stroustrup
books.description: The definitive reference from C++'s creator covering the language core, standard library, and C++11/14/17 features.

books.title: C++ Primer
books.author: Stanley B. Lippman, Josée Lajoie, Barbara E. Moo
books.description: Comprehensive introduction to C++ covering C++11 features in depth. The go-to book for learning modern C++ from scratch.

books.title: Effective Modern C++
books.author: Scott Meyers
books.description: 42 specific ways to improve your use of C++11 and C++14 covering auto, smart pointers, move semantics, lambdas, and concurrency.

books.title: Effective C++
books.author: Scott Meyers
books.description: 55 specific ways to improve your programs and designs covering resource management, design declarations, and OOP in C++.

books.title: C++ Templates: The Complete Guide
books.author: David Vandevoorde, Nicolai M. Josuttis, Douglas Gregor
books.description: Definitive coverage of templates from basics through advanced metaprogramming, variadic templates, and C++17 features.

books.title: Concurrency in Action
books.author: Anthony Williams
books.description: Practical guide to multithreading and concurrency in C++ covering threads, locks, atomics, lock-free programming, and C++20 features.

books.title: A Tour of C++
books.author: Bjarne Stroustrup
books.description: A concise overview of modern C++ (C++20) covering the language and standard library in ~250 pages. Great for experienced programmers.

books.title: Clean C++
books.author: Stephan Roth
books.description: Write maintainable, robust, and readable C++ code following SOLID principles, design patterns, and best practices.

books.title: Programming: Principles and Practice Using C++
books.author: Bjarne Stroustrup
books.description: A gentle introduction to real-world programming using C++ by the language creator. Covers fundamentals through advanced concepts.

books.title: Real-Time C++
books.author: Christopher Kormanyos
books.description: Efficient, optimized C++ for embedded and real-time systems covering memory, performance, and hardware interaction.

projects.title: Custom STL Container (ArrayView)
projects.description: Implement a std::span-like non-owning view over contiguous arrays with iterator support, bounds checking, and constexpr compatibility.

projects.title: JSON Library from Scratch
projects.description: Build a complete JSON parser and serializer supporting all JSON types, with streaming parser and pretty-printing.

projects.title: Lock-Free Thread-Safe Queue
projects.description: Implement a multi-producer, multi-consumer lock-free queue using atomic operations and CAS with proper memory ordering.

projects.title: 2D Game Engine Core
projects.description: Build core engine components: ECS architecture, sprite rendering, input handling, audio, and scene management.

projects.title: Expression Template Math Library
projects.description: Create a linear algebra library using expression templates for lazy evaluation and compile-time optimization.

projects.title: Coroutine-based Network Server
projects.description: Build an async TCP echo server using C++20 coroutines with simple request/response handling.

projects.title: Unit Test Framework (Mini GoogleTest)
projects.description: Implement a minimal testing framework with TEST macros, assertions, test fixtures, and reporting.

projects.title: Huffman Compression Tool
projects.description: Implement file compression using Huffman coding with frequency analysis, tree building, encoding, and binary output.

videoResources.title: Back to Basics: C++ Memory Model

bestPractices: Prefer standard library facilities (std::vector, std::string) over raw arrays and C-style strings
Use RAII for all resource management — never manually manage resources with raw new/delete in application code
Use smart pointers (unique_ptr as default, shared_ptr only for shared ownership)
Prefer nullptr over NULL or 0
Use const whenever possible — const variables, const member functions, const parameters
Mark overriding functions with override (C++11) to catch signature mismatches at compile time
Use pass-by-value for cheaply copyable types, const reference for larger types, move for temporaries
Prefer enum class (C++11) over plain enum for type safety and scoping
Use range-based for loops instead of index-based loops when iterating containers
Follow Core Guidelines — use GSL (Guidelines Support Library) where applicable
Prefer auto for type deduction when the type is obvious from context
Use noexcept for functions that don't throw (enables optimizations, strengthens contract)
Initialize all variables — prefer {} initialization over () or = to avoid most-vexing-parse
Write constexpr whenever computation can happen at compile time
Use std::array instead of C-style arrays for fixed-size sequences
Enable compiler warnings (-Wall -Wextra -Wpedantic -Werror) and use sanitizers
Organize code with header files (.hpp) and implementation files (.cpp); use #pragma once
Keep interfaces narrow — expose only what's necessary, use private/protected for internals
Use move semantics to eliminate unnecessary copies, especially in hot paths
Profile before optimizing — use compiler optimizations (-O2/-O3) and benchmark systematically

careerOpportunities.description: Write and maintain C++ code, fix bugs, write unit tests, and learn the codebase under senior mentorship.
Build rendering pipelines, physics engines, audio systems, and tooling for game engines like Unreal Engine.
Write firmware for microcontrollers, real-time operating systems, device drivers, and hardware interfaces.
Build ultra-low-latency trading systems, market data handlers, order management, and risk systems.
Develop operating systems, file systems, databases, compilers, browsers, or container runtimes.
Work on perception, localization, planning, and control systems for self-driving cars.
Create and maintain C++ libraries, build tools, compilers, static analyzers, and developer tooling.
Develop core database engine components including query execution, storage engine, and transaction processing.
